summary: O'Neil challenges the framing of pension critiques as right-wing attacks, arguing that progressives must engage with fiscal realities rather than cede that territory. She draws parallels between Detroit's potential bankruptcy and how airlines used bankruptcy to shed pension obligations, predicting a similar pattern for cities. The core argument is about unequal application of law: banks received bailouts despite causing their own crises, while ordinary workers and retirees face broken promises. She questions why legal contracts are sacred for some parties but disposable for others.
key_points: Refusing to discuss pension math realities cedes the conversation to those who will act on it
Detroit's bankruptcy may establish a legal template for cities to void pension obligations, similar to how airlines used bankruptcy
The law is applied unequally: banks get bailouts and bonuses while workers get broken contracts
The original bank bailouts violated the principle that failures should fail and criminals should face consequences
Contract sanctity is selectively enforced based on who holds power
money_quotes: If people on the left refuse to discuss reality, then who owns reality?
For whom do laws work? Who can trust that, when they enter a legal obligation, it will be honored?

It's all very well to rest our arguments on the sanctity of the contract, but if you look around the edges you'll see whose contracts get ripped up because of fraudulent accounting, and whose bonuses get bigger.
If we bailed out the banks, why not the people of Detroit?
I'm not a huge fan of the original bailouts, because it ignored the social and legal contracts in the opposite way, that failures should fail and people who are criminals should go to jail.
key_insight: The selective enforcement of contracts and bailouts reveals that legal protections serve the powerful while ordinary workers' pension promises are treated as disposable.
